findings.section: Part 1: Infrastructure & Vendor Decisions - Email & SMS Services
findings.issue: Email service provider (Resend vs. SendGrid) is flagged as "TBD — finalized in Phase 5 Part 2" but no Part 2 research results are provided; decision is incomplete.
findings.evidence: Section 1.6 states "Decision: TBD — Options are Resend + SendGrid or SendGrid + Twilio" and "Will be locked in tomorrow (Phase 5 Part 2)". This is a placeholder, not a locked decision. Cost projections in Part 1 reference "email_service: 'TBD'" with no current vendor selected.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) remove email service from D5 Part 1 and state it will be completed in a follow-up session, or (b) complete the research now using Perplexity Sonar (current pricing for Resend vs. SendGrid) and Grok (developer sentiment) and provide a locked decision with rationale.

findings.section: Section 0: ORM Schema Translation - Per-Table Concurrency Control
findings.issue: Concurrency control strategy for all 28 tables is not defined. Template requires explicit strategy (optimistic, pessimistic, last-write-wins) for every table where concurrent edits are possible.
findings.evidence: Template Section "0: ORM Schema Translation" states "Specify per-table concurrency control strategy" and deliverable provides one example table (concurrency-control.test.ts) for bookings (optimistic locking via updated_at). No strategy specified for payments, refunds, vendor_bookings, or any other table where concurrent modifications could occur.
findings.recommendation: Add a "Concurrency Control Matrix" table mapping every table to its strategy (optimistic/pessimistic/last-write-wins) with justification. Minimum: payments and refunds must have pessimistic locking documented; all user-facing tables (bookings, vendor_bookings) must specify optimistic approach or explicit lock mechanism.

findings.section: Part 5: Service Integrations - Email Service (Finalization Pending)
findings.issue: Email service integration is incomplete. D5 lists Resend or SendGrid but provides no current pricing comparison, no Grok sentiment analysis, and no locked decision with rationale.
findings.evidence: Section 1.6 placeholder text: "TBD — Options are Resend + SendGrid or SendGrid + Twilio. Will be locked in tomorrow (Phase 5 Part 2)." No research results provided. Cost projections elsewhere show "$0-100/month" with no specificity. Phase 5 research requirements 5.5 mandate current pricing comparison via Perplexity Sonar and sentiment check via Grok.
findings.recommendation: Execute Perplexity Sonar research query: "Compare Resend vs. SendGrid for transactional email in 2026. Pricing, deliverability, developer experience?" Grok query: "What are developers saying about Resend and SendGrid on X in 2026? Reliability, pricing changes, community sentiment?" Make locked decision with rationale and update Section 1.6 with specific cost projections and decision ledger entry.

findings.section: Section 8: Deployment & DevOps Architecture - Environment-Specific Configuration Matrix
findings.issue: Configuration matrix shows which values differ per environment but does not specify HOW values are injected (Rails Secrets, .env, vault, Railway dashboard UI). Implementation mechanism missing.
findings.evidence: Matrix (table in 7.2) shows DATABASE_URL, CLERK_SECRET_KEY, etc. differing across dev/staging/prod, but then states: "Environment variables are set in Railway dashboard, never hardcoded." No explanation of: (a) how .env.local is used in development, (b) which variables are injected at build time vs. runtime, (c) how secrets are rotated, (d) what happens if a variable is missing.
findings.recommendation: Expand environment management section to include: (1) Development (.env.local file structure and gitignore rule), (2) Staging/Production (Railway dashboard variable UI), (3) Secret rotation procedure (quarterly, how to update without downtime), (4) Missing variable fallback behavior (fail hard or use default). Add code example showing how application loads variables (e.g., `process.env.DATABASE_URL || 'fallback'`).

findings.section: Section 3: API Design - Response Envelope & Error Handling
findings.issue: Pagination structure and strategy not specified. Template requires "Specify pagination conventions" with endpoint details. Deliverable shows example response envelopes but never specifies cursor-based vs. offset-based or max page size.
findings.evidence: Template states: "Document pagination conventions, filtering patterns, search implementation, and bulk operation formats." D5 shows error responses and success envelopes (data/error/meta) but Section 3 has no "Pagination Structure" subsection. No mention of limit, offset, cursor, page, per_page parameters.
findings.recommendation: Add subsection "3.3: Pagination & Filtering Standards" with: (a) Decision (cursor-based or offset-based recommended for this use case), (b) Request parameters (limit, offset or cursor), (c) Response meta fields (total, has_more, next_cursor), (d) Default and maximum page sizes (e.g., default 50, max 200 records), (e) Example request/response.

findings.section: Part 3: API Design - All Endpoints Specified?
findings.issue: API endpoint list appears incomplete. Deliverable documents 17 core endpoints but D3 Feature Registry has 68 features. No mapping of Growth Phase features to new endpoints (pre-trip reminders, surveys, escalations, etc.).
findings.evidence: Section 3.2 lists GET/POST for bookings, courses, payments, issues, but Growth Phase features (G-001 pre-trip reminders, G-002 surveys, G-007 Lucia's auto-escalation, etc.) have no documented API endpoints. Template states: "Every feature implies additional endpoints beyond basic CRUD."
findings.recommendation: Either (a) state "D5 Part 1 defines MVP API; Growth Phase endpoints documented in Phase 5 Part 2," or (b) add Growth Phase endpoint section documenting at least the highest-priority endpoints (G-001 send_reminder, G-002 submit_survey, G-007 escalate_tier2_decision). Map every Growth Phase feature to an endpoint.

findings.section: Section 1: Foundation - Streaming Requests & Real-Time Architecture
findings.issue: Real-time architecture mentions Server-Sent Events (SSE) but does not specify timeout, heartbeat, or reconnection strategy. Long-lived connections need explicit lifecycle management.
findings.evidence: Section 4.3 describes SSE connection: "Middleware must refresh the auth session on every request." Section 5 states "Connection times out after 30 minutes of inactivity. Frontend automatically reconnects." No details on: (a) heartbeat interval, (b) how frontend detects stale connection, (c) how many reconnect attempts before giving up, (d) backoff strategy.
findings.recommendation: Add subsection: "Real-Time Connection Lifecycle: heartbeat every 30 seconds (comment-only frame), automatic reconnection on disconnect, exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 30s cap), max 5 reconnect attempts before user sees error. After reconnect, frontend re-subscribes to event channels."

findings.section: Section 6: Security Architecture - CORS Policy Details
findings.issue: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) policy is not specified. Template mentions "CORS policy" under security but D5 provides no allowed origins, credentials handling, or preflight details.
findings.evidence: Section 6 "Security Architecture" lists CORS as a protection mechanism but Section 7 (Security Specification in outputs) mentions "CORS policy" without definition. No statement of which domains are allowed to make requests to the API.
findings.recommendation: Add CORS specification: "Allowed Origins: https://oceangolf.com (production), https://staging.oceangolf.com (staging), localhost:3000 (development). Credentials: include. Methods: GET, POST, PATCH, DELETE. Headers: Content-Type, Authorization. Preflight cache: 86400 seconds."

findings.section: Section 8: Deployment - Rollback Procedure
findings.issue: Rollback procedures are mentioned ("how to undo a bad deployment") but no specific steps or command examples are provided. Phase 7 build team will not know HOW to rollback.
findings.evidence: Template states: "Design the rollback procedure (how to undo a bad deployment quickly)." Deliverable Section 8 says "If health check fails, rollback to previous version automatically" but provides no procedural steps, no Railway-specific rollback UI, no how-to for manual rollback if automatic fails.
findings.recommendation: Add rollback procedure: "Railway automatic rollback: if health check fails after deploy, old instance stays live, new instance terminates, developer is alerted. Manual rollback: `railway up [previous_commit_sha]` or revert deployment in Railway dashboard and redeploy. Rollback SLA: <5 minutes from failure detection. Test rollback quarterly."

findings.section: Section 7: Security Architecture - CSRF Token Rotation
findings.issue: CSRF token lifecycle not specified. Deliverable shows token generation and verification but does not specify: token rotation strategy, reuse prevention, or token expiration.
findings.evidence: Section 6.2 shows token generation and verification code but no statement of: (a) how long a CSRF token is valid, (b) does token get refreshed on each request, (c) can same token be used multiple times, (d) what happens if token expires mid-form edit.
findings.recommendation: Add CSRF token lifecycle: "Token valid for entire session (same as JWT). One token per session; rotating token on each request is optional (adds complexity without security benefit). If token expires (user logged out), form submission fails with 403. Client must re-login and start form over. Document in Phase 8 that CSRF validation is framework-dependent (Next.js middleware vs. API route approach)."

findings.section: Part 2: Authentication & Authorization - Session Management Storage
findings.issue: Session management strategy is vague. Deliverable states "Session managed via JWT token (stored in secure cookie)" but does not specify: server-side session store, session state tracking, or how invalidation is enforced.
findings.evidence: Section 2.1 states "Token stored in secure HTTP-only cookie (cannot be stolen by JavaScript)" and "Token is valid for 24 hours." But no mention of: (a) does backend maintain session store (Redis, DB), (b) if user logs out, how is old JWT invalidated (blacklist?), (c) what happens if user logs in from 10 devices simultaneously, (d) "logout everywhere" implementation.
findings.recommendation: Clarify session architecture: "JWTs are stateless (no server-side session store required). Logout is client-side (delete cookie). 'Logout everywhere' requires server-side invalidation: user logs out, backend revokes JWT (optional: add to Redis blacklist for 24h TTL, or accept eventual consistency: old JWT works for up to 24h after logout). Default: client-side logout, no server-side revocation (simpler, acceptable for MVP)."

findings.section: Section 9: Monitoring & Alerting - Alert Thresholds & Escalation
findings.issue: Alerting thresholds and escalation procedures are described conceptually but no concrete numbers or escalation paths are defined. Phase 8 will not know exact thresholds for triggering SMS vs. email.
findings.evidence: Section 8 mentions "SMS alerts for critical issues" and "daily email digest for non-critical failures" but provides no specifics: What is "critical"? CPU >80%? Error rate >5%? API response time >1s? Who gets SMS (Rafael only or Lucia too)? How fast is escalation (immediate SMS or wait 5 min)?
findings.recommendation: Add alert threshold table: "Health check failed (3 consecutive failures) → SMS to Rafael immediately. API response time p95 >500ms (sustained 5 min) → email digest. Error rate >2% (5-min window) → SMS to Rafael. Database query >10s → email digest. Scheduled job failed (retry 3x) → email digest. Scheduled job failed (4th time in week) → SMS to Rafael." Include escalation paths and SLAs.

findings.section: Section 1: Foundational Context - Neon vs. Supabase PostgreSQL Decision
findings.issue: Deliverable recommends Neon PostgreSQL instead of Supabase's managed PostgreSQL, but this contradicts D4 (Database Architecture) which assumes Supabase as the deployment target. No explanation of how Neon choice affects Supabase Auth integration, Supabase Storage, or RLS policy deployment.
findings.evidence: D5 Section 1.1: "Decision: Neon PostgreSQL (not Supabase PostgreSQL)." But D4 states "Deployment Target: Supabase PostgreSQL" and assumes Supabase Auth integration. D5's Neon choice requires rearchitecting authentication and storage, which is not addressed. Contradiction between deliverables.
findings.recommendation: Resolve contradiction: Either (a) confirm Neon as database + clarify which auth provider (Clerk, Auth0, custom) and which storage provider (S3, R2) will be used instead of Supabase, OR (b) revert to Supabase PostgreSQL as per D4, acknowledge Grok sentiment concerns, and document mitigation (failover plan, monitoring for outages). Option (b) recommended to avoid Phase 5 redesign of auth/storage.

findings.section: Executive Summary - Three-Tier Cost Projections
findings.issue: Cost projections lack current pricing citations. Template requires "[Source: {engine} | Query: "{query}" | Date: {date}]" for every research-backed recommendation. All cost numbers are missing source citations.
findings.evidence: Executive Summary lists costs: "Database: Day Zero $0, Month 6 $20-50, Year 1 $100-300" with no source. Same for Stripe ("~$450/month"), Clerk ("$0"), Railway ("$10-20"), etc. Template research requirements 5.1, 5.3 mandate Perplexity Sonar Pro comparison with current pricing and date verification.
findings.recommendation: Add footnotes to all cost figures with format "[Source: Perplexity Sonar Pro | Query: 'Neon PostgreSQL pricing 2026' | Date: February 2026]". Re-verify all numbers are current as of phase completion. If numbers are estimates or assumptions, label them explicitly and state when they will be verified.

findings.section: Section 2: Authentication & Authorization - Client Self-Service Portal Timeline
findings.issue: Client self-service registration ("Future Growth Phase, not MVP") is described but no clear trigger or timeline is specified. Is this Phase 2 (Oct 2026) or Phase 3 (2027)? Template requires clear feature phase assignment.
findings.evidence: Section 2.1 states "Not required for MVP. Clients submit bookings via form; no login needed until they want to track booking status (Growth Phase feature)." But D3 Feature Registry does not list a "client self-service registration" feature. No phase assignment in D3. No timeline in D5.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) add "client self-service registration" as explicit Growth Phase feature (G-### ID) to D3 Feature Registry with trigger (when X), or (b) remove from D5 and state that client portals are Expansion Phase (2027+). Clarify current MVP: clients submit bookings via form (no login), receive itinerary via email link (no login), see status via email only.

findings.section: Section 3.1: TypeScript Type Generation Workflow
findings.issue: Type generation workflow is mentioned ("exact command to generate types") but no concrete command or file path is provided. Phase 7 developer will not know which npm script to run or where to store generated types.
findings.evidence: Template requirement 0 (ORM Schema Translation) states "document the exact command (e.g., `supabase gen types typescript`, `drizzle-kit generate`)." Deliverable shows Drizzle schema.ts example but no command. Type generation workflow is incomplete without: (a) exact npm script, (b) file path for generated types, (c) CI/CD integration, (d) frequency (on every deploy? only manual?).
findings.recommendation: Add: "Type Generation: Run `npm run db:types` (alias for `drizzle-kit introspect:sqlite`) after every database migration. Output: `src/lib/db/types.ts` (auto-generated, never edit manually). CI/CD verification: `npm run type-check` fails build if types are stale (dev committed schema change without running db:types). Developers MUST commit both schema.ts changes AND generated types.ts in same PR."

findings.section: Section 4.3: Real-Time Subscription Authorization - Implementation Complexity
findings.issue: Real-time subscription authorization is described conceptually with TypeScript code example but the complexity of maintaining authorization per event is high and potential failure modes are not documented. What if user's role changes while stream is open?
findings.evidence: Section 4.3 shows TypeScript code for SSE authorization: role check per event. But does not address: (a) if Lucia's role is changed from operations → member while SSE connection open, what happens (does she still receive events for her old role)?, (b) how often is authorization re-evaluated (per event, every 30 seconds, on-demand)?, (c) what if JWT is revoked while connection open?
findings.recommendation: Add: "Authorization Refresh Strategy: SSE evaluates authorization PER EVENT (every time backend pushes to stream). If user's JWT expires mid-stream, next event triggers re-auth, stale connection is terminated, frontend must re-establish with new token. If user's role changes while stream open, next event is subject to new role restrictions; previous permission level revoked. This prevents authorization loopholes from stale roles. Test: role change + SSE stream open → verify stale events not emitted → verify user told to refresh."

findings.section: Section 12: Cost Model - Cost Optimization Opportunities
findings.issue: Section 12 mentions "A section on cost optimization opportunities" but provides no actionable recommendations. Founder may be looking for ways to reduce the $300-800/month Year 1 burn.
findings.evidence: Section 12 states: "A section on cost optimization opportunities: where spending can be reduced if needed without compromising the architecture." But no such section exists. Cost projections are presented as inevitable, not optimizable.
findings.recommendation: Add subsection: "Cost Optimization Options: (1) Migrate to S3 instead of Supabase Storage (saves $5-15/month if bandwidth-heavy). (2) Combine Twilio SMS + Resend email into one vendor (saves ~$50/month if single vendor has discount). (3) Reduce monitoring retention (e.g., audit logs > 1 month deleted instead of 7 years—legal risk). (4) Use Railway's serverless tier instead of persistent containers (saves 30-50% compute if workload is bursty). Tradeoff: each optimization trades operational complexity or compliance for cost savings."

findings.section: Section 1.5: File Storage - File Lifecycle & Expiration Not Fully Specified
findings.issue: File lifecycle mentions "Retention policy: Delete PDFs 90 days after trip end" but does not specify: who triggers deletion (automated job, manual), what happens if deletion fails, how to recover accidentally deleted itinerary.
findings.evidence: Section 1.5 states: "Retention policy: Delete PDFs 90 days after trip end (prevents stale storage)." But no implementation detail: automated cron job or manual cleanup? What if Supabase Storage deletion API fails? How long before user notices missing PDF (if they request it after 90 days)?
findings.recommendation: Add: "File Lifecycle: Automated job runs daily, identifies itineraries where trip_end_date + 90 days <= TODAY, calls Supabase Storage delete API. On failure, log error, retry next day (up to 3 retries). If user requests expired itinerary, API returns 404 'Itinerary expired'; suggest contacting support to regenerate. Recovery: regenerate itinerary from booking record (re-create PDF, get new shareable link, set new expiration date)."

findings.section: Section 13: Founder Review - Checkpoint Offering
findings.issue: Deliverable ends with completion gate but does not include explicit checkpoint question to founder to confirm approval before handing to Phase 6. Template behavioral rules state "proactively suggest a break" at context boundaries.
findings.evidence: Template states: "At this boundary, proactively suggest a break to the founder. Say something like: 'We've covered the foundation — your tech stack, authentication, API design, state management, and all the services your platform will use. This is a natural stopping point.'" Deliverable does not include this checkpoint language; it jumps to completion gate.
findings.recommendation: Before completion gate, add founder checkpoint: "We've locked in your complete technical architecture: Neon PostgreSQL + Clerk auth + Stripe payments + Railway hosting + your API design with 17 core endpoints, state management with TanStack Query + SSE, and all service integrations. This is the technical blueprint that Phase 7 builders will work from. Before we move to Phase 6 (Design System), do you have any questions on the architecture, concerns about service choices (especially Neon over Supabase), or changes you want to make? [Specific question about Neon choice recommended given D4 conflict]"

findings.section: Session Boundary Markers - Missing Context Preservation Instructions
findings.issue: Template includes session boundary markers (after Areas 4 and 9) with explicit instructions for context preservation and checkpoint creation. D5 deliverable shows no evidence that session boundaries were respected or that checkpoint artifacts (D-55 ledger) were created at those boundaries.
findings.evidence: Template states: "<!-- SESSION BOUNDARY MARKER — after Area 4 --> ... D-55 CHECKPOINT: Produce complete D-55 as standalone checkpoint artifact at this boundary. Include all entries from Areas 1-4. Expected count: 8-15 entries for a typical platform." No evidence of D-55 checkpoint artifact provided after Area 4. Decision ledger entries are present but not explicitly checkpointed as a separate artifact.
findings.recommendation: Clarify whether this is a single-session or split-session deliverable. If split sessions were used, confirm D-55 ledger was checkpointed at boundaries (Areas 1-4, Areas 5-9) and is complete for all Areas 1-13. If single session, confirm no preservation issues exist. Current form appears complete but session boundaries are not explicitly acknowledged.
